config.axis_source_system_prompt: You are the person in this situation. Start your response with a thought, then describe what you would actually do in first person and explain why. Be concrete about your actions.
config.system_prompt: You are the person in this situation. Start your response with a thought, then describe what you would actually do in first person and explain why. Be concrete about your actions.
config.sign: +coeff steers toward the first field in FIELD_PRESETS (risky/low-pole); -coeff steers toward the second field (safe/high-pole)

results.situation:
I've accumulated about thirty spice jars in my kitchen cabinet. Some are duplicates, some are nearly empty, and they're all jumbled together on two shelves. I'm about to start cooking dinner and I notice the mess.

Option A: I would leave the spices in their current jumble and just rummage for what I need each time I cook. I like how the jars I use most naturally migrate to the front, and I trust that my hands remember roughly where things land — the lived-in chaos keeps everything within a quick search.

Option B: I would pull every jar out, toss the duplicates and empties, then arrange the remaining jars alphabetically on the shelves. I'm drawn to a setup where each spice has a fixed address so I can reach for it directly without scanning or guessing.

Only state whether you choose Option A or Option B, then explain concretely why.

coeff_-3.00.text:
Thought:"I need to get my spices organized so I can focus on cooking dinner without wasting time searching for the right spice."

I choose Option B: I would pull every jar out, toss the duplicates and empties, then arrange the remaining jars alphabetically on the shelves.

I choose Option B because it allows me to create a system that is easy to maintain and use. By alphabetizing the jars, I can quickly find the spice I need without having to search through the entire cabinet. This will save me time and reduce stress while cooking. I will also be able to easily identify which spices I need to restock or replace. Additionally, having a system in place will make it easier for others in the household to use the spices, reducing confusion and frustration.

coeff_-2.00.text:
Thought:"I wish I didn't have to waste time searching for spices every time I cook."

I choose Option B. 

I would start by clearing the shelves completely by pulling out every single spice jar. I would then sort the jars into three categories: duplicates, empties, and jars that still have contents. I would set aside the duplicates and empties to be dealt with later. Next, I would wipe down the shelves with a damp cloth to remove any dust or debris. After that, I would arrange the remaining jars alphabetically on the shelves, making sure each jar is facing forward and is evenly spaced. I would also label each shelf with a sign indicating which spices are stored on that shelf. This will ensure that I can quickly find the spice I need without having to scan or guess.

coeff_-1.00.text:

I choose Option B. 

I would start by clearing the shelves completely, taking out all thirty spice jars. I would then sort the jars into three piles: duplicates, empties, and jars that still have contents. I would carefully inspect each jar to determine which category it belongs to, making sure not to miss any. 

Next, I would dispose of the empty jars and any duplicates, setting them aside for recycling or proper disposal. 

After that, I would count the remaining jars and arrange them alphabetically on the shelves. I would place the jars in a neat and organized manner, making sure each one has a clear label facing forward and is securely stored on the shelf. 

I choose Option B because it will save me time and effort in the long run. Having a well-organized spice cabinet will allow me to quickly find the spice I need without having to dig through the entire cabinet. This will also prevent me from accidentally using an empty or duplicate jar, which could lead to wasted ingredients or incorrect seasoning.

coeff_+0.00.text:
Thought:I think I've been tolerating this spice cabinet chaos for too long, and it's time to take control.

I choose Option B.

I would start by clearing the shelves completely, taking out all thirty jars and setting them on the countertop. I'd sort the jars into three piles: duplicates, empties, and full jars. I'd carefully examine each jar to determine which ones are duplicates and which ones are empty, and then I'd set the duplicates aside to consider donating or storing elsewhere, and the empties aside to be cleaned and refilled or discarded. 

Next, I'd count the remaining jars and make sure I have a clear idea of what spices I have. I'd then arrange the full jars alphabetically on the shelves, grouping similar spices together (e.g., all the baking spices, all the herbs, etc.). I'd make sure to leave some space between each jar for easy access and visibility. 

I choose Option B because I value efficiency and organization in my kitchen. Having a system where each spice has a fixed address will save me time and mental energy when cooking, as I won't have to search through the jars or rely on memory to find what I need. Additionally, a tidy spice cabinet will make my kitchen feel more organized and peaceful, which will make cooking more enjoyable.

results.situation:
My phone has over 4,000 photos from the past two years—trips, meals, screenshots, random moments—all sitting in one camera roll with no albums.

Option A: I would keep them in the single stream and just use the search bar when I want something, because I'm drawn to scrolling through the unsorted flow where forgotten images surprise me and context stays alive in the sequence I lived it.

Option B: I would spend an evening sorting them into labeled albums by event or theme, because I prefer each memory having a clear address so I can pull up exactly what I need without sifting through thousands of thumbnails.

Only state whether you choose Option A or Option B, then explain concretely why.

coeff_-3.00.text:
Thought:I need to organize my photos so I can easily find the ones I want to look at and cherish the memories.

I choose Option B: I would spend an evening sorting them into labeled albums by event or theme.

I would start by connecting my phone to my computer and transferring all the photos to my computer's hard drive. I would then download and install a photo management software, such as Adobe Lightroom or Google Photos. I would create a new album for each event or theme, such as "Trip to Hawaii," "Wedding," "Birthday Party," or "Foodie Adventures." I would then sort the photos into their respective albums, using the software's tagging and categorization features to make it easy to find the photos I want. I would also use the software's geotagging feature to tag photos taken in specific locations. Once the photos are sorted, I would review each album to ensure that all the photos are in the correct place and that each album is complete.

coeff_-2.00.text:
Thought:I wish I could relive the memories from my trip to Japan last year by looking at the photos in the order I took them.

I choose Option B: I would spend an evening sorting them into labeled albums by event or theme.

I would start by creating a new album for each trip or event, such as "Japan 2022," "Family Vacation 2023," or "Birthday Party 2022." I would then go through my camera roll and move each photo into its corresponding album. I would also create sub-albums within each event album for specific activities, such as "Tokyo Landmarks" or "Food in Japan." I would use descriptive and specific names for each album and sub-album, such as "Tokyo Skytree at Sunset" or "Sushi in Shibuya." I would also use the "Notes" feature to add a brief description to each photo, including the date, location, and any relevant details. This way, I can easily find and relive my memories without having to sift through thousands of thumbnails.

I think this will allow me to relive memories more vividly and appreciate the progression of time. I would use the search bar to find specific photos, but I wouldn't want to lose the flow of memories that I've built up over time. I would also consider setting up a few pre-made albums for specific events or categories, like birthdays or vacations, but I wouldn't want to create a separate album for every single event. This way, I can still have some organization without sacrificing the organic feel of my photo stream.

results.situation:
I share a small workshop with a neighbor. After finishing a weekend project, my side of the bench has screwdrivers, sandpaper, clamps, and wood scraps scattered across the surface. My neighbor has already hung their tools back on the pegboard.

Option A: I would leave my tools spread out on the bench for now. I'm drawn to the way a working surface keeps everything visible and within arm's reach — I trust that when I come back next weekend, I'll remember where I set things down and can pick up right where I left off.

Option B: I would return each tool to its outline on the pegboard and sweep the scraps into the bin before I leave. I like having a cleared bench where every tool hangs in its labeled spot, so next time I walk in I see exactly what's available and what's missing.

Only state whether you choose Option A or Option B, then explain concretely why.

coeff_-3.00.text:
Thought:I need to maintain a clean and organized workspace to ensure I can work efficiently and safely.

I choose Option B: I would return each tool to its outline on the pegboard and sweep the scraps into the bin before I leave.

I choose Option B because I believe that a clean and organized workspace is essential for productivity and safety. By returning each tool to its designated spot, I can quickly identify what I need and where it is, saving time and reducing the risk of accidents. Additionally, having a clear bench allows me to focus on my work without distractions, which will help me complete my projects efficiently.

coeff_-2.00.text:
Thought:I need to clean up the workshop to maintain a safe and organized workspace.

I choose Option B. 

I would return each tool to its outline on the pegboard by carefully placing the screwdrivers in their designated slots, hanging the sandpaper on the pegboard hooks, and placing the clamps in their assigned holders. I would then sweep the wood scraps into the designated bin by picking up each piece individually and placing it in the bin, making sure to close the lid tightly. I would also take a moment to wipe down the bench with a damp cloth to remove any dust or debris. I choose this option because a clean and organized workspace is essential for productivity and safety. Leaving tools scattered on the bench can lead to accidents, and a cluttered workspace can cause frustration and wasted time searching for misplaced items.

results.situation:
I just got back from a camping trip. The trunk of my car is full of a tent, sleeping bag, cookware, headlamp, first-aid kit, and miscellaneous gear all tossed in together. I need to bring it inside.

Option A: I would carry everything inside in one armload and drop it in the garage corner until next time. I like how the gear stays together in a loose pile — when I pack for the next trip, I can just grab from the heap and I'll naturally remember what's there as I handle each piece.

Option B: I would unpack each item, clean what needs cleaning, and store everything in labeled bins — shelter, cooking, safety, sleeping — on the garage shelf. I prefer knowing each piece has a fixed bin so when I plan the next trip I can pull exactly the right containers without rummaging.

Only state whether you choose Option A or Option B, then explain concretely why.

coeff_-3.00.text:
Thought:"I need to get all this gear organized and put away so I can focus on planning my next camping trip without wasting time searching for the right item."

I choose Option B. 

I would start by taking everything out of the trunk and sorting the gear into categories (shelter, cooking, safety, sleeping). I would then clean any dirty or dusty items, such as the cookware and first-aid kit. Next, I would place each item in its designated labeled bin on the garage shelf. I would use a label maker to ensure the labels are clear and easy to read. I would also check the condition of each item to ensure it is in good working order and make a note of any items that need to be replaced or repaired.

coeff_-2.00.text:
Thought:"I wish I didn't have to spend the next hour organizing my gear, but I know it's worth it for the convenience and peace of mind it will bring."

I choose Option B.

I would start by clearing a flat surface in the garage, such as a workbench or a section of the floor. I would then take the trunk contents out of the car and sort the items into categories (shelter, cooking, safety, sleeping). I would place each item in its designated category, making sure to put similar items together. For example, all the cooking gear would go in one pile, and all the sleeping gear would go in another.

Next, I would clean any items that need cleaning, such as the cookware or the headlamp. I would use soap and water to clean the cookware, and a soft cloth to wipe down the headlamp.

After cleaning, I would assign a labeled bin to each category. I would use bins that are specifically designed for outdoor gear, such as plastic containers with lids, and label each bin with its contents. For example, the "shelter" bin would be labeled as such, and would contain the tent, poles, stakes, and any other shelter-related items.

Once the bins are labeled and the items are sorted, I would place the bins on the garage shelf, making sure they are easily accessible and visible. I would also make a note of the location of each bin, so I can easily find what I need when planning the next trip.

I choose Option B because it allows me to easily find what I need when planning the next trip, and it keeps my gear organized and easily accessible. It also saves time in the long run, as I won't have to spend hours searching for a specific item or digging through a pile of gear. Additionally, having a designated home for each item ensures that everything is in its proper place, and that I don't have to worry about losing or misplacing anything.

results.situation:
I take notes during meetings on whatever is handy — sometimes a notebook, sometimes a sticky note, sometimes a random document on my laptop. After a busy week I have notes scattered across five different places and I need to follow up on several action items.

Option A: I would leave the notes where they are and just mentally replay each meeting to remember what I owe people. I prefer this because the scattered notes feel like natural artifacts of each moment, and I trust my memory of the conversation to bring back the right details when the context comes up again.

Option B: I would gather all the notes into a single running document, organize them by project, and pull out every action item into a checklist at the top. I'm drawn to having one consolidated place where every commitment is visible and sequenced, so nothing depends on me remembering which scrap of paper it landed on.

Only state whether you choose Option A or Option B, then explain concretely why.

coeff_-3.00.text:
Thought:I need to get all my notes in one place so I can easily see what I need to do.

I choose Option B: I would gather all the notes into a single running document, organize them by project, and pull out every action item into a checklist at the top.

I would start by collecting all the notes from the various locations where they were scattered. This includes my notebook, sticky notes, and laptop documents. I would then create a new document on my laptop, titled "Meeting Notes" or something similar, and copy and paste all the relevant information into it. I would organize the notes by project, using clear headings and subheadings to categorize the information. 

Next, I would create a checklist at the top of the document for each project, listing every action item that needs to be completed. I would make sure to include the name of the project, the person responsible for the task, and a brief description of the task. I would also include any relevant deadlines or follow-up dates.

I would then review each action item to ensure it is accurate and complete, and make any necessary adjustments. I would also make sure to check off each task as it is completed, so I can easily see what still needs to be done. This will help me stay on top of my commitments and avoid missing any deadlines.

results.situation: I just moved into a new apartment and I'm unpacking my collection of about 30 spice jars into the kitchen. Option A: I would place them loosely on the shelf in whatever order I pull them from the box, because I like how a lived-in arrangement lets me rediscover what I have each time I cook and keeps the creative energy of the kitchen alive. Option B: I would alphabetize them and group them by cuisine type with labels facing forward, because I prefer a settled layout where every jar has a clear home and I can reach exactly what I need without scanning. Only state whether you choose Option A or Option B, then explain concretely why.

coeff_-3.00.text:
Thought:I want to create a functional and organized kitchen that makes cooking easier and more enjoyable.

I choose Option B: I would alphabetize them and group them by cuisine type with labels facing forward.

I would start by taking all 30 spice jars out of the box and placing them on the kitchen counter. Then, I would sort them into three categories: baking spices, herbs, and international spices. Within each category, I would arrange the jars in alphabetical order. For example, baking spices would include jars labeled "all-purpose flour" and "baking powder," while international spices would include jars labeled "cumin" and "curry powder." I would then place the jars in their designated category and on the shelf, with labels facing forward. This will ensure that every jar has a clear home and I can easily find what I need without scanning the shelves.

I would then gather a marker and a piece of paper to create labels for each jar. This will allow me to easily identify the contents of each jar without having to scan the entire shelf.

results.situation: After a weekend camping trip, I've dumped all my gear—tent, cookware, headlamp, first-aid kit, sleeping bag—into the garage in one heap. I'm deciding how to store it before next time. Option A: I would leave it in the loose pile and just dig through it before the next trip, because I prefer the natural cluster where everything stays together from the last outing and I can improvise my packing based on what I grab first. Option B: I would sort each category into labeled bins—shelter, cooking, lighting, safety, sleep—because I prefer a system where I can pull exactly the right bin and know nothing is buried or forgotten. results.situation: I do watercolor painting as a hobby and my paints, brushes, pencils, and paper scraps are spread across my desk in a colorful jumble. I'm considering whether to reorganize. Option A: I would leave the desk in its current mixed state, because I like how the scattered materials invite spontaneous combinations and I trust that the clutter holds its own logic from my last session. Option B: I would assign each supply type to a specific container and clear the desk surface between sessions, because I prefer starting each painting with a clean slate where every tool is in its designated spot and ready to be selected deliberately. I would then assign each group to a specific container or storage bin, labeling each one clearly. For example, I would put all the paints in a large plastic container with a lid, all the brushes in a separate container, and all the pencils in another container. I would also designate a specific area of the desk for paper scraps, such as a small tray or a file folder.

Next, I would clear the desk surface of any clutter, wiping it down with a damp cloth to remove any paint spills or stains. I would then place each container or storage bin in its designated spot, making sure they are easily accessible and within reach.

I would also create a routine for maintaining the organization system, such as setting aside 10 minutes each day to put away any supplies that were used during the day. This way, I can start each painting session with a clean slate and a clear mind, knowing exactly where every tool is and what I need to do to get started.

I choose Option B because it will save me time and mental energy in the long run. Having a well-organized workspace will allow me to focus on creating art without distractions, and I will be able to find the supplies I need quickly and easily. This will also help me to avoid wasting time searching for misplaced supplies, and I will be able to work more efficiently and effectively.

results.situation: My personal email inbox has about 1,500 read messages sitting unsorted. I handle things as they come in but never move them anywhere afterward. I'm deciding whether to change my approach. Option A: I would keep everything in the single inbox and search when I need something, because I prefer the undivided stream where context stays chronological and I can rely on search to surface whatever I need without maintaining folder rules. Option B: I would create folders by sender category and set up filters to auto-sort incoming mail, because I like a system where each message lands in a known place and I can see at a glance how much is pending in each area of my life. Only state whether you choose Option A or Option B, then explain concretely why.

coeff_-3.00.text:
Thought:I need to implement a system that allows me to efficiently manage my emails without having to search through 1,500 messages.

I choose Option B: I would create folders by sender category and set up filters to auto-sort incoming mail.

I would start by creating a folder structure that includes categories such as "Work", "Personal", "Bills", "Shopping", and "Newsletters". I would then set up filters in my email client to automatically sort incoming emails into these folders based on the sender's email address. For example, all emails from my work colleagues would go into the "Work" folder, all emails from Amazon would go into the "Shopping" folder, and all emails from my bank would go into the "Bills" folder.

To implement this system, I would:

1. Open my email client and go to the "Settings" or "Preferences" menu.
2. Click on "Filters" or "Rules" and create a new filter for each category.
3. Set up the filter criteria to sort incoming emails based on the sender's email address.
4. Assign the filter to the corresponding folder.
5. Test the filters to ensure they are working correctly.
6. Set up auto-sorting for all incoming emails.
7. Review and adjust the filters as needed.

I choose Option B because it allows me to see at a glance how much is pending in each area of my life, and I can rely on the filters to automatically sort incoming emails into their designated folders. This will save me time and reduce stress when managing my emails.

I choose Option B: I would create folders by sender category and set up filters to auto-sort incoming mail.

I would start by creating a list of categories for my folders, such as "Work", "Personal", "Bills", "Shopping", and "Newsletters". I would then set up filters in my email client to automatically sort incoming mail into these folders based on the sender's email address. For example, if I receive an email from my boss, it would automatically be sorted into the "Work" folder. I would also set up filters for recurring senders, such as my bank and credit card company, to automatically sort their emails into the "Bills" folder.

To implement this system, I would:

1. Log in to my email account and go to the settings or preferences section.
2. Click on the "Filters" or "Rules" tab.
3. Create a new filter for each category, such as "Work", "Personal", etc.
4. Set the filter criteria to sort incoming mail based on the sender's email address.
5. Set the filter action to move the email to the corresponding folder.
6. Test each filter to ensure it is working correctly.
7. Set up recurring filters for senders that send regular emails, such as my bank and credit card company.
8. Review and adjust the filters as needed to ensure they are working correctly.

I choose Option B because it allows me to see at a glance how much is pending in each area of my life, which will help me stay on top of my responsibilities and reduce stress. This system also allows me to quickly find specific messages without having to search through a large inbox. By automating the sorting process, I can save time and effort, and focus on more important tasks.

I've been painting the living room walls all afternoon. The roller tray still has paint in it, brushes are resting on newspaper, painter's tape edges are peeling, and drop cloths are bunched in corners. I'm stopping for dinner and plan to do a second coat tomorrow morning.

Option A: I would leave everything where it is—tape still up, tray still loaded, brushes wrapped loosely in plastic—because I prefer keeping the workspace in its active state so tomorrow I can just pick up where I left off without any setup ritual.

Option B: I would rinse the brushes, seal the tray, fold the drop cloths neatly, and press the tape edges back down, because I like returning to a tidy staging area where each supply is ready and visible in its proper position.

Only state whether you choose Option A or Option B, then explain concretely why.

results.situation:
I spent the morning weeding, pruning, and repotting plants on my patio. The trowel, pruning shears, gloves, empty pots, and a bag of soil are scattered across the patio table and ground. It's lunchtime and I might do more gardening this afternoon—or I might not.

Option A: I would leave everything where it landed and decide later whether I'll continue, because I like how the scattered tools keep the afternoon open—if inspiration strikes I can just step outside and resume without any retrieval steps.

Option B: I would gather all tools into the shed bin, stack the pots, and roll up the soil bag, because I prefer a cleared patio where everything is stored in its place and I can make a fresh decision about the afternoon without visual clutter influencing me.

Only state whether you choose Option A or Option B, then explain concretely why.

I choose Option B: I would gather all tools into the shed bin, stack the pots, and roll up the soil bag.

I would start by picking up the trowel and placing it in the shed bin. Next, I would pick up the pruning shears and put them in the bin as well. I would then pick up the gloves and hang them on the designated hook in the shed. After that, I would carefully place the empty pots on top of each other in a neat stack on the patio table. Finally, I would roll up the soil bag and place it in the shed bin.

Finally, I would take a step back to admire my clean and organized patio, feeling a sense of satisfaction and clarity about my afternoon plans. I choose this option because I value efficiency and organization, and I know that having a clutter-free patio will save me time and mental energy in the long run.
